nativeRange.habitat: Open-water column and sandy shallows of Lake Malawi; associated with Vallisneria beds in sheltered bays and the lake’s littoral zone; ambush predator that hovers motionless among plant stems before striking
nativeRange.biotope: Lake Malawi open water / Vallisneria beds

decorPreferences: large open swimming volume is the priority
fine sand or mixed sand-gravel substrate
Vallisneria plants or artificial equivalents to replicate natural ambush habitat
minimal rockwork — rocks can be used at tank ends for territorial dividers
robust, fast-growing plants only (softer plants will be uprooted)

diet.notes: The Malawi Eyebiter is an obligate piscivore in the wild, documented to target the eyes of prey fish — biting them out to blind and immobilise the victim before swallowing it whole. In captivity it generally accepts large carnivore pellets, frozen silversides, whole krill, large mysis, lance fish, and other meaty frozen or prepared foods. Live feeder fish are not necessary and carry disease risk. This species does not accept plant-based or algae-based foods. Feed large meaty items two to three times per week and avoid overfeeding, as this species can become obese in captivity.

goodWith: very large Lake Malawi haplochromines of comparable size (20 cm+)
large Lake Malawi peacock cichlids (Aulonocara, large male-only groups)
large robust Lake Malawi mbuna if tank is very large (400 L+)
similarly-sized Synodontis catfish as dither fish
avoidWith: any fish that fits in its mouth — will be eaten
other Dimidiochromis compressiceps males in standard tanks (extreme conspecific aggression)
community fish, tetras, danios, livebearers, or any small freshwater species
Lake Tanganyika or Amazon cichlids (incompatible water chemistry and temperament)
Altolamprologus compressiceps (fw-fish-167) — a completely unrelated Tanganyika species sharing the “Compressiceps” trade name

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Dimidiochromis compressiceps; verify current URL on fishbase.se species page before use.
Wikimedia Commons entry for D. compressiceps; confirm license and photographer attribution on page before use.

summary: The Malawi Eyebiter (Dimidiochromis compressiceps) is a large, laterally compressed cichlid from Lake Malawi, reaching 25 cm and named for its documented habit of biting out the eyes of prey fish. Males develop vivid metallic blue coloration. An intermediate-level predator suited only to large, species-appropriate Malawi aquariums.
careNotes:
Dimidiochromis compressiceps is one of Lake Malawi’s most specialised open-water predators. Its extreme lateral compression — similar in appearance to the unrelated Tanganyikan Altolamprologus compressiceps (fw-fish-167) — is an adaptation for slipping through stands of Vallisneria grass while ambushing prey. When hunting, the fish tends to hang motionless, aligned with plant stems, before striking with explosive speed.

NAMING COLLISION — CRITICAL: The trade name “Compressiceps” is shared by two completely unrelated cichlids from different lakes with incompatible care requirements. The Malawi Eyebiter, Dimidiochromis compressiceps, is a Lake Malawi haplochromine: an open-water predator reaching 25 cm, kept in water of pH 7.7–8.6, and a maternal mouthbrooder. The other species, Altolamprologus compressiceps (fw-fish-167), is a Lake Tanganyika lamprologine: a rocky-littoral crevice predator reaching 15 cm, kept in harder, more alkaline water (pH 8.2–9.0), and a cave or shell spawner. The two require different water chemistry and entirely different aquarium setups. Always confirm the scientific name when purchasing — the trade name “Compressiceps” alone is not sufficient to identify which species you are buying.

Eyebiting predation: The common name “Eyebiter” derives from the species’ documented habit of attacking the eyes of prey fish — biting them out to blind and immobilise the victim before consuming it. This behaviour has been observed in the wild against juvenile cichlids and is reported in captivity when conspecifics or smaller fish are kept together. Any tankmate small enough to be targeted is at genuine risk of injury or death, not merely “being eaten”.

Size and tank requirements: Adults reach 20–25 cm; males are typically larger than females and develop vivid metallic blue coloration at breeding condition. A minimum 400 L tank with a footprint of at least 150 cm is recommended to provide adequate swimming volume and territory. This is a mid-water cruiser — vertical height matters less than horizontal length. Sparse decor with large open midwater is preferred over densely decorated Malawi rock setups.

Water chemistry: Lake Malawi is hard and alkaline (pH 7.7–8.6, moderate hardness). Target the middle of these ranges for stability. The species is less demanding about extreme alkalinity than Tanganyika endemics and is more forgiving of slight pH drift, but acidic or soft water will cause rapid health decline. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are important given the large bioload of this predator.

Diet in captivity: This species does not need live feeders. High-quality frozen silversides, whole krill, lance fish, and large sinking carnivore pellets are all accepted. Live feeders introduce disease risk and are unnecessary. Avoid feeder goldfish (high fat, thiaminase). Feed moderately — piscivores in captivity can become obese with excess feeding.

Compatibility: Essentially a species-only or very-large-Malawi-only fish. Adult males are highly aggressive toward conspecifics and toward any fish they can attack. A single male in a very large tank with similarly-sized Malawi peacocks and haplochromines is the most viable community. Tankmates must be fast-moving and of similar or larger body size.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. The male establishes a spawning pit or territory in open sand and courts females with intense color display. After egg deposition and fertilisation, the female collects the eggs in her buccal cavity and broods them for approximately 21–28 days without feeding. Fry are released as fully formed juveniles capable of feeding on baby brine shrimp nauplii and finely crushed foods immediately. Females should be removed to a separate tank to brood undisturbed — harassment by the male or other tankmates causes premature spitting. Breeding is not particularly difficult once a compatible pair is established in a sufficiently large tank, but the predatory adult males present a genuine risk to fry and juveniles if not separated. The main challenges are establishing a compatible pair without injury and safely separating fry from the predatory adults.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dimidiochromis compressiceps
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania), water parameters (pH 7.7–8.6, temperature 24–28°C, hardness 143–321 ppm), adult size (to 25 cm TL), diet (piscivore, eye-predation behaviour documented), habitat (Vallisneria beds, open water), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day brooding period), compatibility notes, and minimum tank size guidance.

additional.name: FishBase — Dimidiochromis compressiceps
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, genus Dimidiochromis), adult TL (to 25.8 cm), native range (Lake Malawi basin: Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania), depth range (1–15 m), temperature range (24–28°C), and eye-predation behaviour. Confirmed this is a distinct genus from Altolamprologus compressiceps (Lake Tanganyika), resolving the common-name collision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Lake Malawi Cichlid Care
additional.notes: Trade-focused Lake Malawi care guide. Confirmed water parameter targets (pH 7.8–8.5, temperature 76–82°F / 24–28°C, hard water), tank size requirements for large haplochromines, diet recommendations (avoid feeder goldfish, use frozen carnivore foods), aggression management, and compatibility grouping guidance for large Malawi predators.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Dimidiochromis compressiceps profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed eye-predation behaviour (documented field observation and captive reports), adult male blue coloration and sexual dimorphism, Vallisneria ambush hunting strategy, mouthbrooding reproductive mode, and intermediate care rating. Also addressed common name confusion with Altolamprologus compressiceps from Lake Tanganyika.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Malawi Eyebiter
additional.notes: TFH reference covering Dimidiochromis compressiceps husbandry and natural history. Confirmed open-water predator ecology, extreme lateral compression as ambush adaptation, eyebiting predation mechanism and behavioural context, sexual dimorphism (males to 25 cm, vivid blue; females smaller and drabber), maternal mouthbrooding, and species-appropriate tank community guidelines. Notes naming clash with Altolamprologus compressiceps (Lake Tanganyika).

fish.breedingNotes: The Malawi Eyebiter is a maternal mouthbrooder. After spawning, the female collects the eggs and broods them in her mouth for approximately 21–28 days without feeding. The brooding female should be moved to a separate tank to avoid harassment from the male or other tankmates. Fry are released as fully formed juveniles and accept baby brine shrimp nauplii immediately. The main challenges are establishing a compatible pair without aggression and safely separating fry from the predatory adults.
